title: Why correlate k6 results with metrics, logs, and traces

blocks.content:
A passing load test is easy to celebrate. It is harder to show the system was ready, not just the script: k6 answers for your controlled client, while users and probes see other paths, regions, caches, and dependencies. When those stories disagree, thresholds alone do not tell you which one to trust.

- **Spot the launch-confidence gap.** Green checks mean the scenario you modeled behaved. They do not prove synthetic monitors stayed healthy everywhere, that a new error class skipped the logs, or that latency hid in a few hot spans. Walking metrics, logs, and traces in one UTC window shows the space between "the test passed" and "we ship to customers tomorrow."
- **Shorten the story after the spike.** Teams replay the same questions in reviews: what broke first, did recovery lag, did errors cluster with traffic? If you already used Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo for the same window as k6, you bring a short, evidence-backed narrative instead of a second day of ad hoc digging, and product and leadership get a clearer go, no-go, or go-with-fixes call.
- **Practice the loop you reuse in reviews.** Fix the test window in UTC, compare external probes to generator metrics, classify errors in logs, then explain latency with traces so the next readiness conversation ties business risk to concrete signals, not only to pass or fail lines on a k6 summary.

Check your work: you can move on when you can state in one sentence why thresholds alone are not enough for a launch decision, and what you gain by walking metrics, logs, and traces in one UTC window.

In the next milestone you step into the quickpizza scenario and the investigation plan you use for every step.
